subject: reading
title: Grade 3 Reading — Middle of Year (MOY) Test 2
instructions: Read each selection carefully. Some questions have two parts or ask you to use both parts of the text. Choose the best answer for each question.

passages.title: The Mural on Oak Street
passages.text:
Part 1

The brick wall at the end of Oak Street had been gray and plain for as long as Lena could remember. Every day she walked past it on her way to school, and every day it seemed a little more dull. One morning she stopped and stared at the empty wall. "That wall could be beautiful," she said out loud.

Lena drew a picture of a giant garden full of bright flowers and painted birds. She carried her drawing to the community center and showed it to Ms. Park, who ran the neighborhood meetings. "This is wonderful," Ms. Park said, "but a wall this big needs many hands. One painter alone would take a whole year."

Lena did not give up. She made small flyers and taped them to mailboxes and shop windows up and down the street. The flyers asked neighbors to help paint a mural on the gray wall. At the first meeting, only two people came, and Lena felt a flutter of worry in her chest.

Part 2

The next Saturday was different. Word had spread from neighbor to neighbor. When Lena arrived at the wall, she could not believe her eyes. Almost twenty people stood waiting with brushes, buckets, and old shirts. Mr. Diaz brought ladders from his shop. Two teachers brought leftover paint from the school.

The neighbors worked all weekend. Children painted the small flowers near the bottom, while taller helpers reached the birds near the top. By Sunday evening, the dull gray wall had turned into a garden that seemed to glow. People driving by slowed down to look and smile.

Lena stood back and felt proud, but she was even happier about something else. The mural had brought the whole street together. Neighbors who had never spoken now waved and laughed like old friends. "A wall can do more than hold up a building," Lena thought. "This one holds up our whole neighborhood."

passages.title: The Ant and the Grasshopper
passages.text:
Part 1

All through the warm days of summer, a grasshopper hopped through the meadow, singing and playing his fiddle. The grass was green, the air was sweet, and food was easy to find. "Why work," he chirped, "when the sun is bright and the world is full of food?"

Nearby, a small brown ant marched back and forth, carrying grains of wheat that were nearly as big as she was. Drop by drop, the sweat rolled off her, but she did not stop. She stored each grain deep inside her hill, building up a pile for later.

"Come and play with me!" called the grasshopper. "Summer will last forever." The ant shook her head. "Winter is coming," she answered. "I am gathering food now so that I will have enough when the snow falls." The grasshopper only laughed and hopped away to play another tune.

Part 2

The golden days of summer slowly faded. The leaves turned brown and dropped from the trees. Then the cold winds blew, and snow covered the meadow in a thick white blanket. There was no green grass and no food to be found anywhere.

The grasshopper grew thin and hungry. He shivered in the icy wind, wishing he had something to eat. At last he crept to the ant's warm hill and begged for a meal. The ant remembered the summer, but she was kind. She shared a little of her stored food so the grasshopper would not starve.

"Thank you," said the grasshopper, ashamed of how he had spent his summer. "I see now that it is wise to prepare for hard times before they come." The ant nodded. From that day on, the grasshopper worked beside her, and he never laughed at hard work again.

passages.title: How Spiders Spin Their Webs
passages.text:
Part 1

Many people are surprised to learn that spiders are not insects. Insects have six legs, but spiders have eight. Spiders also have a special talent that most insects do not have: they can make silk. This silk is one of the strongest materials in nature, and spiders use it to build webs that catch food.

A spider makes silk inside its body using tiny organs called spinnerets. The silk starts as a liquid, but as the spider pulls it out, the silk turns into a solid thread. The thread is so thin that it is hard to see, yet it is stretchy and very strong. Some spider silk is stronger than a steel thread of the same size.

To begin a web, a spider must build a bridge. It releases a long thread of silk and lets the wind carry it across a gap. When the thread sticks to a branch or wall on the other side, the spider has its first line. From this single line, the whole web will grow.

Part 2

Next, the spider builds a frame and adds spokes that reach out from the center, like the lines on a wheel. These first threads are not sticky, so the spider can walk on them safely. They give the web its shape and hold everything in place.

Then the spider spins a sticky spiral around the spokes, starting from the center and circling outward. This sticky silk is the part that traps insects. When a fly bumps into the web, it gets stuck, and the spider hurries out to catch its meal. Because the spider walks only on the dry threads, it does not get caught in its own trap.

A web does not last forever. Wind, rain, and struggling insects can tear it apart. Many spiders build a fresh web each day. Instead of letting the old silk go to waste, some spiders eat it and use it to make new silk. In this way, a spider can spin a brand-new web again and again.

questions.questionText: According to the text, why does Lena stop and stare at the brick wall one morning?
options: She is lost on her way to school.
She wants to climb over the wall.
She thinks the wall could be beautiful.
She is waiting for Ms. Park.
questions.explanation: In Part 1, Lena stares at the empty wall and says out loud, "That wall could be beautiful."

questions.questionText: What did Lena do after Ms. Park said the wall needs many hands?
options: She painted a smaller wall instead.
She gave up on the mural.
She decided to paint the wall by herself.
She made flyers asking neighbors to help.
questions.explanation: After Ms. Park said the wall needs many hands, Lena made flyers and taped them to mailboxes and shop windows to ask neighbors to help.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the phrase "a flutter of worry in her chest" in paragraph 3 of Part 1?
options: Lena feels nervous because only two people came.
Lena is angry at her neighbors.
Lena is too tired to keep working.
Lena is excited that so many people came.
questions.explanation: Only two people came to the first meeting, so "a flutter of worry" shows Lena feels nervous that not enough people will help.

questions.questionText: According to Part 2, what did Mr. Diaz bring to help with the mural?
options: Brushes and old shirts
Ladders from his shop
Leftover paint from the school
Small flyers
questions.explanation: Part 2 states that Mr. Diaz brought ladders from his shop.

questions.questionText: What is the main theme, or lesson, of this story?
options: Gray walls are always ugly.
Working together can make a community stronger.
Painting is the best way to spend a weekend.
It is hard to find good paint.
questions.explanation: The story ends with the mural bringing the whole street together, showing that working together makes the community stronger.

questions.questionText: Which word best describes Lena based on her actions in the story?
options: Fearful
Lazy
Determined
Selfish
questions.explanation: Even when only two people came, Lena did not give up and kept working to organize the mural, which shows she is determined.

questions.questionText: Which sentence from the text best supports the idea that the mural did more than just decorate a wall?
options: "Lena drew a picture of a giant garden full of bright flowers and painted birds."
"Neighbors who had never spoken now waved and laughed like old friends."
"Children painted the small flowers near the bottom."
"The brick wall at the end of Oak Street had been gray and plain."
questions.explanation: The sentence about neighbors who had never spoken now waving and laughing shows the mural brought people together, which is more than just decorating a wall.

questions.questionText: Use both parts of this text to answer this question. How is the wall different at the end of the story compared to the beginning?
options: It is still gray and plain.
It changed from dull and gray to a bright, glowing garden.
It was torn down and rebuilt.
It is taller than it was before.
questions.explanation: Part 1 describes the wall as gray, plain, and dull, while Part 2 says the neighbors turned it into a garden that seemed to glow.

questions.questionText: According to Part 1, what does the grasshopper do all through the summer?
options: He builds a hill to store food.
He sleeps in the shade.
He gathers grains of wheat.
He sings and plays his fiddle.
questions.explanation: Part 1 says the grasshopper hopped through the meadow singing and playing his fiddle all summer.

questions.questionText: Why does the ant work so hard during the summer?
options: She wants to win a race against the grasshopper.
She is gathering food for the winter.
She is building a fiddle of her own.
She does not know how to play.
questions.explanation: The ant tells the grasshopper she is gathering food now so she will have enough when the snow falls.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the word "faded" in the sentence "The golden days of summer slowly faded" in Part 2?
options: Grew brighter
Became warmer
Stayed the same
Came to an end
questions.explanation: The days "faded" as the leaves turned brown and winter arrived, so "faded" means the summer slowly came to an end.

questions.questionText: What happens to the grasshopper after the snow covers the meadow?
options: He flies south for the winter.
He finds plenty of food to eat.
He builds his own hill.
He grows thin and hungry.
questions.explanation: Part 2 says the grasshopper grew thin and hungry and shivered in the icy wind with no food to find.

questions.questionText: Which word best describes the ant in the story?
options: Hardworking
Careless
Greedy
Foolish
questions.explanation: The ant works without stopping all summer to store food, which shows she is hardworking.

questions.questionText: What lesson does this fable mainly teach?
options: It is best to play all the time.
Singing is more important than working.
It is wise to prepare for hard times before they come.
Ants and grasshoppers cannot be friends.
questions.explanation: The grasshopper learns it is wise to prepare for hard times before they come, which is the lesson of the fable.

questions.questionText: Use both parts of this text to answer this question. Why is the grasshopper ashamed at the end of the story?
options: He could not find his fiddle in the snow.
He played all summer instead of storing food, and now he must beg.
He ate all of the ant's food without asking.
He sang a song that the ant did not like.
questions.explanation: In Part 1 the grasshopper played while the ant worked, and in Part 2 he must beg the ant for food, so he is ashamed of how he spent his summer.

questions.questionText: Select the two details from the story that show the ant was getting ready for winter.
options: She stored each grain deep inside her hill.
She played a tune on a fiddle.
She carried grains of wheat that were nearly as big as she was.
She hopped through the meadow singing.
questions.explanation: The ant carried grains of wheat and stored each one in her hill to prepare for winter. Playing the fiddle and hopping while singing are things the grasshopper did.

questions.questionText: What is the main idea of this text?
options: Spider silk is weaker than steel.
Spiders are scary and dangerous animals.
Spiders use silk to build webs in careful steps.
All insects can make silk.
questions.explanation: The whole text explains how spiders make silk and use it to build webs step by step.

questions.questionText: According to the text, how many legs do spiders have?
options: Eight
Ten
Six
Four
questions.explanation: The text says insects have six legs, but spiders have eight.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the word "spinnerets" in paragraph 2 of Part 1?
options: Tiny organs a spider uses to make silk
Insects that get caught in webs
Branches where webs are built
Sticky threads at the center of a web
questions.explanation: The text says a spider makes silk inside its body using tiny organs called spinnerets.

questions.questionText: What step does a spider take first when building a web?
options: It builds a bridge by letting the wind carry a thread across a gap.
It adds spokes that reach out from the center.
It spins a sticky spiral around the spokes.
It eats its old web.
questions.explanation: Part 1 explains that to begin a web, a spider first builds a bridge by releasing a thread that the wind carries across a gap.

questions.questionText: According to the text, why does the spider NOT get stuck in its own web?
options: Its legs are covered in water.
Its silk is too weak to hold the spider.
It walks only on the dry, non-sticky threads.
It never touches the web at all.
questions.explanation: Part 2 says the spokes are not sticky and the spider walks only on the dry threads, so it does not get caught in its own trap.

questions.questionText: Select the two facts the text gives about spider silk.
options: It is made by all insects.
It can never be torn apart.
It starts as a liquid inside the spider.
It can be stronger than a steel thread of the same size.
questions.explanation: The text states the silk starts as a liquid and that some spider silk is stronger than a steel thread of the same size. It also says webs can be torn by wind, rain, and insects, and that spiders, not insects, make silk.

questions.questionText: How does the author connect the ideas in the paragraph about the sticky spiral and the paragraph about webs not lasting forever in Part 2?
options: The author first explains how a finished web catches food, then explains why a spider must build a new web.
The author tells a made-up story about one spider's day.
The author lists the colors of spider silk.
The author compares two different kinds of spiders.
questions.explanation: The sticky-spiral paragraph explains how the finished web traps insects, and the next paragraph explains why webs wear out and must be rebuilt, connecting how a web works to why spiders make new ones.

questions.questionText: Use both parts of this text to answer this question. What is the connection between Part 1 and Part 2 of the article?
options: Both parts compare spiders to insects.
Part 1 describes a problem and Part 2 gives an opinion about it.
Part 2 happens before Part 1 in time.
Part 1 explains what silk is and how a web begins, and Part 2 gives the next steps to finish and rebuild the web.
questions.explanation: Part 1 explains what spider silk is and how the spider starts a web with a bridge, and Part 2 continues with the frame, spokes, sticky spiral, and rebuilding, giving the steps in order.
